glossary.def: The recursive identity a value function satisfies for a fixed policy: V^π(s) equals the policy-averaged immediate reward plus the discounted value of the successor state. Because V^π appears on both sides, it is a system of |S| linear equations whose unique solution is the value function, and solving it is the task of policy evaluation.

The fixed-point equation the optimal value function satisfies: V*(s) = max_a Σ p(s',r|s,a)[r + γV*(s')]. Replacing the policy average with a maximum over actions makes it nonlinear, but it still has a unique solution from which the optimal policy is read off by acting greedily, π*(s) = argmax_a Q*(s,a).

The prediction step of dynamic programming: computing V^π for a given policy π, usually by iterating the Bellman expectation backup to convergence from any initial guess. It scores a policy but does not improve it; its value is as the inner loop of policy iteration and as the template every model-free prediction method imitates.

A control algorithm that applies the Bellman optimality backup — a single greedy max over actions — to every state each sweep, driving V straight to V* without naming intermediate policies. It is policy evaluation truncated to one step before re-improving, and converges geometrically because the Bellman optimality operator is a γ-contraction.

A control algorithm that alternates full policy evaluation with a full greedy policy-improvement step until the policy stops changing. The policy improvement theorem guarantees each round is monotonically non-worse, and since a finite MDP has finitely many deterministic policies it terminates at the exact optimum in a finite number of rounds.

The mapping T that takes a value table to a better one via one backup; the optimal value V* is its fixed point, T V* = V*. For γ < 1 it is a γ-contraction in the max-norm, ||TU − TV||∞ ≤ γ||U − V||∞, so the Banach fixed-point theorem guarantees a unique fixed point reached from any start — the reason value iteration converges.

The unifying view of nearly all RL control as two interacting processes — evaluation, which makes the value consistent with the policy, and improvement, which makes the policy greedy with respect to the value — chasing each other until they agree, which can only happen at the optimal pair (V*, π*). Value iteration and policy iteration are endpoints of this spectrum.

The guarantee that acting greedily with respect to V^π for one step and then following π yields a new policy π' with V^{π'}(s) ≥ V^π(s) for every state, strictly better somewhere unless π is already optimal. It is what makes the greedy step of policy iteration monotone and is the engine behind generalized policy iteration.

refs.cite: Bellman, R. (1957). Dynamic Programming. Princeton University Press.
Bellman, R. (1957). A Markovian Decision Process. Journal of Mathematics and Mechanics 6(5).
Howard, R. A. (1960). Dynamic Programming and Markov Processes. MIT Press.
Sutton, R. S. & Barto, A. G. (2018). Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction (2nd ed.), Ch. 4. MIT Press.
Puterman, M. L. & Shin, M. C. (1978). Modified Policy Iteration Algorithms for Discounted Markov Decision Problems. Management Science 24(11).
Bertsekas, D. P. (2017). Dynamic Programming and Optimal Control (4th ed.). Athena Scientific.
